title: AI capex by company
question: Who is actually paying for the AI buildout, company by company - how big is each one's capex, how fast is it growing, and is each company funding the build out of its own cash or out of someone else's balance sheet?
finding: It is not a broad boom - it is five companies. Across 45 US-listed filers spanning the whole AI value chain, latest-fiscal-year capex totals about $520bn, but the top five (the hyperscalers - Amazon $132bn, Alphabet $91bn, Meta $70bn, Microsoft $65bn, Oracle $56bn) are 79% of it, Amazon alone a quarter, and their combined capex grew +73% in one year ($238bn -> $413bn). The rest of the chain barely spends: fabless silicon (Nvidia 2.8% capex/revenue, +$97bn FCF) and software (Palantir, Salesforce <2%) monetize the build rather than fund it. The fragility is the funding, not the magnitude: the cash-rich core self-funds (Microsoft 47%, Alphabet 56%, Nvidia 6% of operating cash flow; even Amazon at 95% stays FCF-positive), while a thin, nameable edge spends past its entire operating cash flow - Oracle 174% (-$24bn FCF, the one mega-cap funding the build on debt), CoreWeave 337% (capex 3.4x operating cash), and neoclouds (CIFR/APLD/WULF) running negative operating cash flow with capex at 200-600% of revenue. Verdict conditional: 'AI capex' as a market-wide phenomenon is a myth (it is five spenders plus a levered fringe); the systemic risk lives in that fringe and in Oracle, the names dependent on financing staying open - exactly the financing-refusal trigger of studies 27 and 30, now with names and numbers. Source: SEC EDGAR XBRL, gross PP&E capex, latest full fiscal year. Caveat: US filers only, so the true global total (with TSMC ~$40bn, Samsung, SK Hynix, Alibaba) is materially higher and US-concentration is an overstatement; capex is all PP&E, not AI-only.
